title: RHEED pattern classification by a convolutional neural network for the growth of chalcogenide thin films and nanostructures
summary: The use of reflection high energy electron diffraction (RHEED) plays a critical role for in-situ characterization in molecular beam epitaxy, pulsed laser deposition and sputtering. While sensitive to crystal symmetries and morphology, it is used ubiquitously to determine the growth modes of thin films. However, analysis of RHEED patterns depends on skilled experts and is therefore difficult to incorporate into the growth strategy in real-time. The development of machine learning (ML) processes, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs), presents a unique opportunity towards real-time RHEED pattern recognition. In this study, we develop a CNN model that can accurately classify four common and distinct RHEED patterns present in chalcogenide thin film growth. Its reached accuracy reached 94.9% for single run and 91.2% when averaged over 20 seeds. Our network is able to distinguish the nucleation of three common growth modes encountered in epitaxy, namely Volmer Weber, Stransky-Krastanov and Frank-van der Merwe, potentially enabling future automation of substrate temperature and shutter control informed by RHEED data. The network is material-agnostic and distinguishes the VW process with greater than 98% accuracy but is somewhat more limited in its ability to properly classify roughening and the initiation of Stransky-Krastanov growth. Our findings show that ML techniques can be successfully implemented even in cases where there is no detailed knowledge of growth chemistry providing an avenue towards real-time incorporation of ML to control nanostructure nucleation and thin film morphology.

processed.summary:
- Development of a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** architecture designed to interpret Reflection High Energy Electron Diffraction (RHEED) patterns in real-time.
- The model bridges the gap between expert-dependent manual characterization and **autonomous thin film growth** for chalcogenide materials.
- Focuses on identifying four distinct morphologies, providing a pathway for **real-time shutter and temperature control** in molecular beam epitaxy.
- Employs a **material-agnostic approach** that remains effective without detailed knowledge of specific growth chemistry.
key_results: Reached a peak classification accuracy of 94.9% for a single experimental run.
Maintained a stable average accuracy of 91.2% across 20 different random seeds.
Achieved greater than 98% accuracy specifically for the Volmer-Weber (VW) growth mode identification.
Successfully distinguished between three major epitaxial modes: Volmer Weber, Stransky-Krastanov, and Frank-van der Merwe.
Identified performance bottlenecks in classifying surface roughening and the early initiation of Stransky-Krastanov growth.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Arkansas researchers developed a CNN to classify RHEED patterns, enabling real-time automation of thin film growth modes.

title: Reflections on the Future of Statistics Education in a Technological Era
summary: Keeping pace with rapidly evolving technology is a key challenge in teaching statistics. To equip students with essential skills for the modern workplace, educators must integrate relevant technologies into the statistical curriculum where possible. University-level statistics education has experienced substantial technological change, particularly in the tools and practices that underpin teaching and learning. Statistical programming has become central to many courses, with R widely used and Python increasingly incorporated into statistics and data analytics programmes. Additionally, coding practices, database management, and machine learning now feature within some statistics curricula. Looking ahead, we anticipate a growing emphasis on artificial intelligence (AI), particularly the pedagogical implications of generative AI tools such as ChatGPT. In this article, we explore these technological developments and discuss strategies for their integration into contemporary statistics education.

processed.summary:
- Transition of statistics education from theoretical foundations to a **statistical programming**-centric model utilizing R and Python.
- Systematic integration of **machine learning** and database management practices into core curricula to align with modern data science requirements.
- Evaluation of the pedagogical shift required to incorporate **Generative AI** tools like ChatGPT into assessment and learning workflows.
- Strategic focus on **modernizing data analytics** programs to maintain relevance against evolving technological and industry standards.
key_results: R remains the primary language, but Python is rapidly gaining ground in data analytics curricula.
Machine learning and database management are now essential components of university-level statistics.
Generative AI is identified as the next major disruptor in statistical pedagogy.
Need for educators to adapt to AI-driven coding and problem-solving environments.
Shift toward technology-driven curricula to ensure student workforce readiness.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose integrating generative AI and machine learning into statistics curricula to better prepare students for modern industry.

title: [Re] Benchmarking LLM Capabilities in Negotiation through Scoreable Games
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant potential in multi-agent negotiation tasks, yet evaluation in this domain remains challenging due to a lack of robust and generalizable benchmarks. Abdelnabi et al. (2024) introduce a negotiation benchmark based on Scoreable Games, with the aim of developing a highly complex and realistic evaluation framework for LLMs. Our work investigates the reproducibility of claims in their benchmark, and provides a deeper understanding of its usability and generalizability. We replicate the original experiments on additional models, and introduce additional metrics to verify negotiation quality and evenness of evaluation. Our findings reveal that while the benchmark is indeed complex, model comparison is ambiguous, raising questions about its objectivity. Furthermore, we identify limitations in the experimental setup, particularly in information leakage detection and thoroughness of the ablation study. By examining and analyzing the behavior of a wider range of models on an extended version of the benchmark, we reveal insights that provide additional context to potential users. Our results highlight the importance of context in model-comparative evaluations.

processed.summary:
- Replicates the **Scoreable Games** framework to evaluate LLM performance in complex **multi-agent negotiation** tasks.
- Enhances the benchmark with **additional metrics** to measure negotiation quality and the evenness of model evaluations.
- Critically analyzes **information leakage** and identifies limitations in the original ablation studies and experimental setups.
- Demonstrates that model-comparative evaluations are highly sensitive to **context**, questioning the objectivity of current ranking methods.
key_results: Confirmed the high complexity and realism of the Scoreable Games negotiation benchmark.
Discovered significant ambiguity in model rankings, reducing the benchmark's objectivity.
Detected vulnerabilities in the experimental setup regarding potential information leakage.
Found that original ablation studies were insufficient to verify negotiation quality.
Established that evaluation context drastically influences the perceived performance of LLM negotiators.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM negotiation benchmarks suffer from ambiguous scoring and information leakage, complicating objective model comparisons.

title: Search for a new resonance decaying to a Higgs boson and a scalar boson in events with two b jets and two Z bosons in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13.6 TeV
summary: A search is performed for a new resonance X decaying into either a pair of Higgs bosons (HH) or into a Higgs boson and a new scalar boson Y (HY), using proton-proton collision data collected at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 138 fb$^{-1}$. This study performs a comprehensive exploitation of the bbZZ events, encompassing the following decay topologies. One H candidate is identified through its decay into a bottom quark-antiquark pair, while the other H or the Y candidate is selected through its decay into a pair of Z bosons. One Z boson is required to decay leptonically and the other, to decay into a pair of quarks or neutrinos. Events of interest are categorized based on the Lorentz boosts of the hadronically decaying H and Z bosons. Machine-learning-based discriminants, together with the reconstructed resonance mass, are employed across the different categories to separate signal from backgrounds, and their corresponding distributions are included in a simultaneous fit. No significant deviations from the standard model predictions are observed. Upper limits at the 95% confidence level are set on the HH and HY production cross sections. For resonant HH production, the upper limit on the cross section of pp $\to$ HH production is 1 pb for a high-mass resonance. For HY production, the upper limit on the cross section of the process pp $\to$ X $\to$ bbZZ is approximately 5 fb for a high-mass resonance. This is comparable to the sensitivity achieved in other analyses, which focus on H decays to $γγ$ or $ττ$ and Y decays into a pair of bottom quarks or massive vector bosons.

processed.summary:
- The study employs **machine-learning-based discriminants** to categorize and separate potential new resonance signals (X to HH or HY) from complex Standard Model backgrounds in high-energy physics data.
- The architecture utilizes **Lorentz boosts** of hadronically decaying H and Z bosons as features for the ML models, which are then integrated into a **simultaneous fit** of reconstructed resonance mass distributions.
- This approach enables a comprehensive exploitation of the **bbZZ decay topology**, optimizing signal sensitivity across various leptonic and hadronic decay channels of Z bosons.
- The impact is the establishment of new **upper limits** on the production cross sections of heavy resonances, proving that ML-driven analysis of the bbZZ channel is competitive with traditional photon and tau decay channels.
key_results: Analyzed 138 inverse femtobarns of proton-proton collision data at 13 TeV.
Targeted resonant production of Higgs pairs (HH) and Higgs plus scalar bosons (HY).
Applied ML-based discriminants to enhance signal-to-background separation.
Observed no significant deviations from Standard Model predictions.
Set an upper limit of 1 pb for HH production and 5 fb for HY production at high mass.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The CMS Collaboration utilizes machine-learning discriminants to set new limits on Higgs boson resonance decays in proton-proton collisions.

title: Generative Model via Quantile Assignment
summary: Deep Generative models (DGMs) play two key roles in modern machine learning: (i) producing new information (e.g., image synthesis) and (ii) reducing dimensionality. However, traditional architectures often rely on auxiliary networks such as encoders in Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) or discriminators in Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), which introduce training instability, computational overhead, and risks like mode collapse. We present NeuroSQL, a new generative paradigm that eliminates the need for auxiliary networks by learning low-dimensional latent representations implicitly. NeuroSQL leverages an asymptotic approximation that expresses the latent variables as the solution to an optimal transportation problem. Specifically, NeuroSQL learns the latent variables by solving a linear assignment problem and then passes the latent information to a standalone generator. We benchmark its performance against GANs, VAEs, and a budget-matched diffusion baseline on four datasets: handwritten digits (MNIST), faces (CelebA), animal faces (AFHQ), and brain images (OASIS). Compared to VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models: (1) in terms of image quality, NeuroSQL achieves overall lower mean pixel distance between synthetic and authentic images and stronger perceptual/structural fidelity; (2) computationally, NeuroSQL requires the least training time; and (3) practically, NeuroSQL provides an effective solution for generating synthetic data with limited training samples. By embracing quantile assignment rather than an encoder, NeuroSQL provides a fast, stable, and robust way to generate synthetic data with minimal information loss.

processed.summary:
- **NeuroSQL** introduces a standalone generator paradigm that bypasses traditional auxiliary architectures like **VAE encoders** or **GAN discriminators**.
- It utilizes **quantile assignment** to solve a linear assignment problem, implicitly learning low-dimensional latent representations through **optimal transportation** principles.
- The approach eliminates **training instability** and the risk of **mode collapse** by removing the adversarial or variational overhead typical in existing Deep Generative Models.
- Provides a high-efficiency alternative for **synthetic data generation**, particularly effective in low-resource environments and specialized domains like medical imaging.
key_results: Achieved overall lower mean pixel distance compared to VAEs, GANs, and diffusion baselines.
Demonstrated the fastest training time among all benchmarked generative architectures.
Significantly improved structural fidelity on MNIST, CelebA, AFHQ, and OASIS datasets.
Proved highly effective for generating high-quality synthetic data with limited training samples.
Reduced computational overhead by eliminating the need for auxiliary network optimization during training.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: NeuroSQL researchers develop a quantile-based generative paradigm that outperforms GANs and VAEs in training speed and fidelity.

title: Machine-learning force-field models for dynamical simulations of metallic magnets
summary: We review recent advances in machine learning (ML) force-field methods for Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) simulations of itinerant electron magnets, focusing on scalability and transferability. Built on the principle of locality, a deep neural network model is developed to efficiently and accurately predict the electron-mediated forces governing spin dynamics. Symmetry-aware descriptors constructed through a group-theoretical approach ensure rigorous incorporation of both lattice and spin-rotation symmetries. The framework is demonstrated using the prototypical s-d exchange model widely employed in spintronics. ML-enabled large-scale simulations reveal novel nonequilibrium phenomena, including anomalous coarsening of tetrahedral spin order on the triangular lattice and the freezing of phase separation dynamics in lightly hole-doped, strong-coupling square-lattice systems. These results establish ML force-field frameworks as scalable, accurate, and versatile tools for modeling nonequilibrium spin dynamics in itinerant magnets.

processed.summary:
• Developed a **Deep Neural Network** architecture designed to predict electron-mediated forces for **Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG)** simulations.
• Integrates **symmetry-aware descriptors** via a group-theoretical approach to maintain rigorous lattice and spin-rotation symmetries.
• Leverages the **principle of locality** to achieve high scalability and transferability in modeling itinerant electron magnets.
• Enables the discovery of **nonequilibrium phenomena**, such as phase separation freezing and anomalous coarsening in spin orders.
key_results: Creation of a scalable ML force-field framework for metallic magnets.
Rigorous incorporation of physical symmetries using group-theoretical descriptors.
Successful simulation of large-scale s-d exchange models in spintronics.
Discovery of anomalous coarsening of tetrahedral spin order on triangular lattices.
Identification of frozen phase separation dynamics in hole-doped square-lattice systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop symmetry-aware ML force-fields to simulate large-scale nonequilibrium spin dynamics in itinerant magnets.

title: Art Notions in the Age of (Mis)anthropic AI
summary: In this paper, I take the cultural effects of generative artificial intelligence (generative AI) as a context for examining a broader perspective of AI's impact on contemporary art notions. After the introductory overview of generative AI, I summarize the distinct but often confused aspects of art notions and review the principal lines in which AI influences them: the strategic normalization of AI through art, the representation of AI art in the artworld, academia, and AI research, and the mutual permeability of art and kitsch in the digital culture. I connect these notional factors with the conceptual and ideological substrate of the computer science and AI industry, which blends the machinic agency fetishism, the equalization of computers and humans, the sociotechnical blindness, and cyberlibertarianism. The overtones of alienation, sociopathy, and misanthropy in the disparate but somehow coalescing philosophical premises, technical ideas, and political views in this substrate remain underexposed in AI studies so, in the closing discussion, I outline their manifestations in generative AI and introduce several viewpoints for a further critique of AI's cultural zeitgeist. They add a touch of skepticism to pondering how technological trends change our understanding of art and in which directions they stir its social, economic, and political roles.

processed.summary:
• Examines the **strategic normalization** of generative AI through its integration into contemporary art notions and academic discourse.
• Critiques the **ideological substrate** of the AI industry, identifying a blend of cyberlibertarianism, sociotechnical blindness, and **machinic agency fetishism**.
• Analyzes the **mutual permeability** between high art and digital kitsch resulting from automated generative workflows.
• Explores the **cultural zeitgeist** of AI, highlighting underexposed themes of alienation and misanthropy within technical and philosophical AI premises.
key_results: Identification of 'machinic agency fetishism' as a core ideological driver in AI development.
Analysis of how AI normalization in art obscures the social and political roles of technology.
Evaluation of the blurring lines between creative art and automated kitsch in digital culture.
Critique of the 'equalization' of human and computer agency as a reductive sociotechnical view.
Framework for addressing the misanthropic overtones present in current generative AI trends.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers analyze how generative AI ideologies impact art notions and contribute to sociotechnical alienation in digital culture.

title: SOMtime the World Ain$'$t Fair: Violating Fairness Using Self-Organizing Maps
summary: Unsupervised representations are widely assumed to be neutral with respect to sensitive attributes when those attributes are withheld from training. We show that this assumption is false. Using SOMtime, a topology-preserving representation method based on high-capacity Self-Organizing Maps, we demonstrate that sensitive attributes such as age and income emerge as dominant latent axes in purely unsupervised embeddings, even when explicitly excluded from the input. On two large-scale real-world datasets (the World Values Survey across five countries and the Census-Income dataset), SOMtime recovers monotonic orderings aligned with withheld sensitive attributes, achieving Spearman correlations of up to 0.85, whereas PCA and UMAP typically remain below 0.23 (with a single exception reaching 0.31), and against t-SNE and autoencoders which achieve at most 0.34. Furthermore, unsupervised segmentation of SOMtime embeddings produces demographically skewed clusters, demonstrating downstream fairness risks without any supervised task. These findings establish that \textit{fairness through unawareness} fails at the representation level for ordinal sensitive attributes and that fairness auditing must extend to unsupervised components of machine learning pipelines. We have made the code available at~ https://github.com/JosephBingham/SOMtime

processed.summary:
- Introduces **SOMtime**, a high-capacity **Self-Organizing Map** architecture for topology-preserving unsupervised representation learning.
- Demonstrates that **sensitive attributes** like age and income emerge as dominant latent axes even when withheld from input.
- Challenges the **'fairness through unawareness'** paradigm, showing unsupervised embeddings can encode demographic biases more effectively than PCA or UMAP.
- Emphasizes the need for **AI Evaluation** and fairness auditing to cover unsupervised components of machine learning pipelines.
key_results: Achieved Spearman correlations up to 0.85 with withheld sensitive attributes.
Significantly outperformed PCA, UMAP, and t-SNE which remained below 0.34 correlation.
Validated findings on large-scale World Values Survey and Census-Income datasets.
Demonstrated that unsupervised segmentation produces demographically skewed clusters without a supervised task.
Established that high-capacity SOMs preserve ordinal structures better than traditional autoencoders.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Joseph Bingham shows SOMtime unsupervised embeddings recover withheld sensitive attributes, invalidating the 'fairness through unawareness' assumption.

title: RAT+: Train Dense, Infer Sparse -- Recurrence Augmented Attention for Dilated Inference
summary: Structured dilated attention has an appealing inference-time efficiency knob: it reduces the FLOPs of the attention and the KV cache size by a factor of the dilation size D, while preserving long-range connectivity. However, we find a persistent failure mode of them -- sparsifying a pretrained attention model to a dilated pattern leads to severe accuracy degradation. We introduce RAT+, a dense-pretraining architecture that augments attention with full-sequence recurrence and active recurrence learning. A single RAT+ model is pretrained densely once, then flexibly switched at inference time to dilated attention (optionally with local windows) or hybrid layer/head compositions, requiring only a short 1B-token resolution adaptation rather than retraining separate sparse models. At 1.5B parameters trained on 100B tokens, RAT+ closely matches dense accuracy at 16 and drops by about 2-3 points at 64 on commonsense reasoning and LongBench tasks, respectively. Moreover, RAT+ outperforms attention when sparsifying to the top-k block attention. We further scale to 2.6B parameters and 200B tokens and observe the same trend.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **RAT+**, a novel architecture that combines **dense pretraining** with full-sequence recurrence to enable flexible, sparse inference.
* Utilizes **Recurrence Augmented Attention** to mitigate the accuracy loss typically seen when sparsifying pretrained attention models into dilated patterns.
* Enables inference-time switching between **dilated attention** and hybrid compositions with only a minimal 1B-token resolution adaptation.
* Significantly reduces **inference FLOPs** and **KV cache requirements** by a factor of the dilation size while maintaining long-range connectivity.
key_results: Matches dense model accuracy at a dilation factor of 16 for a 1.5B parameter model.
Maintains performance within 2-3 points of dense models even at a dilation factor of 64 on LongBench.
Demonstrates superior performance over standard attention when using top-k block sparsification.
Successfully scales to 2.6B parameters and 200B tokens with consistent efficiency gains.
Eliminates the need for training separate sparse models for different inference-time efficiency requirements.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce RAT+, an architecture enabling efficient sparse inference with minimal accuracy loss via dense pretraining and recurrence.

title: BLM-Guard: Explainable Multimodal Ad Moderation with Chain-of-Thought and Policy-Aligned Rewards
summary: Short-video platforms now host vast multimodal ads whose deceptive visuals, speech and subtitles demand finer-grained, policy-driven moderation than community safety filters. We present BLM-Guard, a content-audit framework for commercial ads that fuses Chain-of-Thought reasoning with rule-based policy principles and a critic-guided reward. A rule-driven ICoT data-synthesis pipeline jump-starts training by generating structured scene descriptions, reasoning chains and labels, cutting annotation costs. Reinforcement learning then refines the model using a composite reward balancing causal coherence with policy adherence. A multitask architecture models intra-modal manipulations (e.g., exaggerated imagery) and cross-modal mismatches (e.g., subtitle-speech drift), boosting robustness. Experiments on real short-video ads show BLM-Guard surpasses strong baselines in accuracy, consistency and generalization.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **BLM-Guard**, a content-audit framework that integrates **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** reasoning with rule-based policy principles for multimodal ad moderation.
- Features a **rule-driven ICoT data-synthesis pipeline** that generates structured scene descriptions and reasoning chains to minimize manual annotation costs.
- Implements a **Reinforcement Learning** (RL) approach using a **critic-guided reward** to align model outputs with policy adherence and causal coherence.
- Utilizes a **multitask architecture** capable of identifying both intra-modal manipulations and cross-modal discrepancies like **subtitle-speech drift**.
key_results: Surpasses competitive baselines in classification accuracy and reasoning consistency.
Successfully detects subtle cross-modal mismatches in real-world short-video ads.
Significant reduction in manual data annotation requirements via automated synthesis.
Improved generalization capabilities across diverse commercial ad categories.
Enhanced robustness in identifying deceptive imagery and exaggerated visual claims.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce BLM-Guard, utilizing Chain-of-Thought and RL to provide explainable, policy-aligned multimodal moderation for short-video advertisements.

title: A geometric physics-informed machine learning inference for the neutron star maximum mass and the inverse problem
summary: The existence of a distinct mass boundary between the heaviest neutron stars and the lightest black holes remains in question. It is an artefact of our ignorance of the properties of matter at supra-nuclear densities, which exist in the cores of neutron stars. The study addresses these problems with a physics-informed machine learning approach, guided by astrophysical observations. The Transformer model is trained on an agnostically generated ensemble of equations of state. Two geometric parameters are defined on the mass-radius sequence of a neutron star--the front bending and the back bending. The transformer provides a two-step solution: first, the model predicts the maximum mass and radius using the bending parameters. Second, it predicts the square of the sound speed profile, completing the inverse mapping. The prediction is that massive neutron stars form when the sound speed peaks at low density, leading to strong back-bending and an early phase transition to quark matter. Massive stars favour a stiff equation of state at low density, and the density of matter at the star's core is sufficiently small. The maximum mass for a neutron star predicted by the astrophysical constrained transformer model is $2.477$ solar masses, and a minimum radius of about $11.498$ km for a neutron star of $1.4$ solar masses.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **Physics-Informed Machine Learning** (PIML) framework using **Transformer architectures** to solve the inverse problem of neutron star equations of state.
- Introduces novel **geometric parameters**—specifically front and back bending—to characterize mass-radius sequences for more accurate property mapping.
- Executes a **two-step inference pipeline**: first predicting macroscopic mass/radius and then inferring the microscopic **sound speed profile** of nuclear matter.
- Demonstrates the efficacy of **Generative AI** techniques in bridging sparse astrophysical observations with complex supra-nuclear density physics.
key_results: Predicted a maximum neutron star mass of 2.477 solar masses.
Calculated a minimum radius of 11.498 km for a standard 1.4 solar mass neutron star.
Discovered that massive stars favor a stiff equation of state at low densities.
Correlated strong back-bending geometry with early phase transitions to quark matter.
Validated that sound speed peaks at low density are critical for the formation of massive neutron stars.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize Transformer models to predict neutron star mass and internal sound speed via geometric physics-informed learning.

title: Role and Identity Work of Software Engineering Professionals in the Generative AI Era
summary: The adoption of Generative AI (GenAI) suggests major changes for software engineering, including technical aspects but also human aspects of the professionals involved. One of these aspects is how individuals perceive themselves regarding their work, i.e., their work identity, and the processes they perform to form, adapt and reject these identities, i.e., identity work. Existent studies provide evidence of such identity work of software professionals triggered by the adoption of GenAI, however they do not consider differences among diverse roles, such as developers and testers. In this paper, we argue the need for considering the role as a factor defining the identity work of software professionals. To support our claim, we review some studies regarding different roles and also recent studies on how to adopt GenAI in software engineering. Then, we propose a research agenda to better understand how the role influences identity work of software professionals triggered by the adoption of GenAI, and, based on that, to propose new artifacts to support this adoption. We also discuss the potential implications for practice of the results to be obtained.

processed.summary:
• The study explores the **sociotechnical impact** of **Generative AI (GenAI)** on software engineering, specifically focusing on the evolution of **professional identity work**.
• It addresses a research gap where existing studies overlook how **distinct roles** (e.g., developers vs. testers) experience differing identity adaptations when adopting AI tools.
• The authors propose a **research agenda** to map how AI-driven automation alters perceived professional value and the internal processes of identity formation.
• The research focuses on developing **new artifacts** and support mechanisms to facilitate the organizational and psychological transition to the GenAI era.
key_results: Identification of identity work as a critical, overlooked factor in GenAI integration within engineering teams.
Argument for role-based differentiation (developer vs. tester) when analyzing the impact of AI adoption.
Proposal of a structured research agenda for studying professional identity formation and rejection.
Highlighting the need for role-specific artifacts to support the human-AI collaborative transition.
Analysis of the long-term implications for the human-centric aspects of the software development lifecycle.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a research agenda to investigate how GenAI reshapes professional identities and roles within software engineering teams.

title: Capabilities Ain't All You Need: Measuring Propensities in AI
summary: AI evaluation has primarily focused on measuring capabilities, with formal approaches inspired from Item Response Theory (IRT) being increasingly applied. Yet propensities - the tendencies of models to exhibit particular behaviours - play a central role in determining both performance and safety outcomes. However, traditional IRT describes a model's success on a task as a monotonic function of model capabilities and task demands, an approach unsuited to propensities, where both excess and deficiency can be problematic. Here, we introduce the first formal framework for measuring AI propensities by using a bilogistic formulation for model success, which attributes high success probability when the model's propensity is within an "ideal band". Further, we estimate the limits of the ideal band using LLMs equipped with newly developed task-agnostic rubrics. Applying our framework to six families of LLM models whose propensities are incited in either direction, we find that we can measure how much the propensity is shifted and what effect this has on the tasks. Critically, propensities estimated using one benchmark successfully predict behaviour on held-out tasks. Moreover, we obtain stronger predictive power when combining propensities and capabilities than either separately. More broadly, our framework showcases how rigorous propensity measurements can be conducted and how it yields gains over solely using capability evaluations to predict AI behaviour.

processed.summary:
- Introduces a novel **bilogistic formulation** for AI evaluation that shifts focus from raw capabilities to behavioral **propensities**, or the tendency of a model to exhibit specific behaviors.
- Adapts **Item Response Theory (IRT)** by replacing monotonic success functions with an **"ideal band"** framework, where both deficiency and excess in a trait can lead to failure.
- Utilizes LLMs to develop **task-agnostic rubrics** that define the limits of these ideal behavioral bands across different task demands.
- Enhances predictive modeling of AI behavior by integrating **propensity measurements** with traditional capability scores, yielding higher accuracy than either metric alone.
key_results: Established the first formal framework for measuring and quantifying AI behavioral propensities.
Demonstrated that propensity shifts can be precisely measured across six different LLM families.
Proved that propensities estimated on one benchmark successfully generalize to held-out tasks.
Found that successful task performance requires a model's propensity to fall within a specific 'ideal band'.
Showed that combining capability and propensity data provides superior predictive power for model outcomes.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a bilogistic framework to measure AI propensities, significantly improving behavioral prediction over standard capability evaluations.

title: SeedFlood: A Step Toward Scalable Decentralized Training of LLMs
summary: This work presents a new approach to decentralized training-SeedFlood-designed to scale for large models across complex network topologies and achieve global consensus with minimal communication overhead. Traditional gossip-based methods suffer from message communication costs that grow with model size, while information decay over network hops renders global consensus inefficient. SeedFlood departs from these practices by exploiting the seed-reconstructible structure of zeroth-order updates and effectively making the messages near-zero in size, allowing them to be flooded to every client in the network. This mechanism makes communication overhead negligible and independent of model size, removing the primary scalability bottleneck in decentralized training. Consequently, SeedFlood enables training in regimes previously considered impractical, such as billion-parameter models distributed across hundreds of clients. Our experiments on decentralized LLM fine-tuning demonstrate thatSeedFlood consistently outperforms gossip-based baselines in both generalization performance and communication efficiency, and even achieves results comparable to first-order methods in large scale settings.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **SeedFlood**, a decentralized training framework that utilizes **zeroth-order (ZO) optimization** to achieve global consensus with minimal communication overhead.
- Employs a **seed-reconstructible** structure where clients transmit only random seeds instead of full gradient tensors, making message size **independent of model size**.
- Enables **flooding-based communication** across complex topologies, effectively removing the scalability bottlenecks and information decay found in traditional gossip-based methods.
- Facilitates the **decentralized fine-tuning** of billion-parameter models across hundreds of clients, matching the performance of first-order methods in specific large-scale settings.
key_results: Reduces communication overhead to near-zero by flooding random seeds rather than model updates.
Enables scalable training for billion-parameter models across high-latency, complex network topologies.
Outperforms standard gossip-based decentralized baselines in generalization and efficiency.
Maintains competitive performance relative to first-order optimization methods in large-scale regimes.
Eliminates the dependency between model size and communication cost in decentralized environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SeedFlood, utilizing seed-reconstructible zeroth-order updates to scale decentralized LLM training with near-zero communication overhead.

title: High-Fidelity Teleportation of Continuous-Variable Quantum States Via Non-Ideal Qutrit Entangled Resources
summary: Achieving near-unity fidelity in conventional continuous-variable quantum teleportation schemes based on two-mode squeezed vacuum states is fundamentally unattainable. To overcome this limitation, alternative approaches utilizing ensembles of two-dimensional entangled qubits have been proposed. In this work, we investigate continuous-variable quantum teleportation employing entangled qutrit resources under realistic noise effects. The results demonstrate that the proposed scheme performs well in both ideal and noisy conditions, enabling high-fidelity teleportation with a reasonable success probability.

processed.summary:
* The study introduces a **continuous-variable (CV) quantum teleportation** architecture that replaces standard two-mode squeezed vacuum states with **entangled qutrit resources**.
* It addresses the fundamental fidelity limitations of conventional schemes by utilizing **higher-dimensional entanglement** to approach near-unity performance.
* The research specifically models **realistic noise effects**, demonstrating that the qutrit-based approach maintains robustness in non-ideal environmental conditions.
key_results: Surpasses the fidelity benchmarks of traditional two-mode squeezed vacuum teleportation.
Maintains high-fidelity performance even under simulated realistic noise conditions.
Achieves a commercially viable success probability for CV state transmission.
Provides a theoretical framework for utilizing qutrits in continuous-variable systems.
Demonstrates that non-ideal resources can still support high-fidelity quantum communication.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose using entangled qutrits to achieve high-fidelity continuous-variable quantum teleportation in noisy, real-world environments.

title: Improving Sampling for Masked Diffusion Models via Information Gain
summary: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) offer greater flexibility in decoding order than autoregressive models but require careful planning to achieve high-quality generation. Existing samplers typically adopt greedy heuristics, prioritizing positions with the highest local certainty to decode at each step. Through failure case analysis, we identify a fundamental limitation of this approach: it neglects the downstream impact of current decoding choices on subsequent steps and fails to minimize cumulative uncertainty. In particular, these methods do not fully exploit the non-causal nature of MDMs, which enables evaluating how a decoding decision reshapes token probabilities/uncertainty across all remaining masked positions. To bridge this gap, we propose the Info-Gain Sampler, a principled decoding framework that balances immediate uncertainty with information gain over future masked tokens. Extensive evaluations across diverse architectures and tasks (reasoning, coding, creative writing, and image generation) demonstrate that Info-Gain Sampler consistently outperforms existing samplers for MDMs. For instance, it achieves a 3.6% improvement in average accuracy on reasoning tasks and a 63.1% win-rate in creative writing. Notably, on reasoning tasks it reduces cumulative uncertainty from 78.4 to 48.6, outperforming the best baseline by a large margin. The code will be available at https://github.com/yks23/Information-Gain-Sampler.

processed.summary:
• Proposes the **Info-Gain Sampler**, a novel decoding framework for **Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs)** that replaces greedy heuristics with a strategy focused on **cumulative uncertainty minimization**.
• Leverages the **non-causal nature** of MDMs to assess how current token selections provide information gain for all remaining masked positions, optimizing the global decoding path.
• Validates the architecture across multiple domains including **reasoning, coding, and multimodal image generation**, proving the scalability of information-theoretic sampling.
key_results: Achieved a 3.6% average accuracy improvement on complex reasoning benchmarks.
Recorded a 63.1% win-rate in creative writing tasks compared to existing samplers.
Reduced cumulative uncertainty in reasoning tasks from 78.4 to 48.6.
Outperformed state-of-the-art MDM samplers by a large margin across diverse architectures.
Demonstrated significant improvements in token-level probability shaping through future-aware planning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Info-Gain Sampler to optimize Masked Diffusion Model decoding by prioritizing future information gain over greedy local certainty.

title: Can AI Lower the Barrier to Cybersecurity? A Human-Centered Mixed-Methods Study of Novice CTF Learning
summary:
Capture-the-Flag (CTF) competitions serve as gateways into offensive cybersecurity, yet they often present steep barriers for novices due to complex toolchains and opaque workflows. Recently, agentic AI frameworks for cybersecurity promise to lower these barriers by automating and coordinating penetration testing tasks. However, their role in shaping novice learning remains underexplored.
  We present a human-centered, mixed-methods case study examining how agentic AI frameworks -- here Cybersecurity AI (CAI) -- mediates novice entry into CTF-based penetration testing. An undergraduate student without prior hacking experience attempted to approach performance benchmarks from a national cybersecurity challenge using CAI. Quantitative performance metrics were complemented by structured reflective analysis of learning progression and AI interaction patterns.
  Our thematic analysis suggest that agentic AI reduces initial entry barriers by providing overview, structure and guidance, thereby lowering the cognitive workload during early engagement. Quantitatively, the observed extensive exploration of strategies and low per-strategy execution time potetially facilitatates cybersecurity training on meta, i.e. strategic levels. At the same time, AI-assisted cybersecurity education introduces new challenges related to trust, dependency, and responsible use. We discuss implications for human-centered AI-supported cybersecurity education and outline open questions for future research.

processed.summary:
- The study evaluates **agentic AI frameworks** (Cybersecurity AI) to mediate novice entry into complex **offensive cybersecurity** and CTF competitions.
- Researchers utilized a **human-centered mixed-methods** design to analyze how AI-driven **toolchain automation** impacts learning curves and performance.
- Findings show that AI agents facilitate **meta-strategic learning** by offloading low-level execution, though they introduce risks regarding **trust and dependency**.
- The interaction patterns suggest a shift from manual command execution to **high-level orchestration** and strategy evaluation.
key_results: Agents significantly reduce cognitive workload by providing structural guidance in opaque workflows.
Novices achieved higher rates of strategic exploration compared to traditional manual methods.
AI interaction significantly lowered the time required for per-strategy execution.
Identified a shift towards meta-level training where learners focus on strategy over syntax.
Identified critical risks regarding over-reliance and misplaced trust in AI-generated solutions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that agentic AI frameworks lower cybersecurity entry barriers by reducing cognitive load and automating workflows.

title: Click it or Leave it: Detecting and Spoiling Clickbait with Informativeness Measures and Large Language Models
summary: Clickbait headlines degrade the quality of online information and undermine user trust. We present a hybrid approach to clickbait detection that combines transformer-based text embeddings with linguistically motivated informativeness features. Using natural language processing techniques, we evaluate classical vectorizers, word embedding baselines, and large language model embeddings paired with tree-based classifiers. Our best-performing model, XGBoost over embeddings augmented with 15 explicit features, achieves an F1-score of 91\%, outperforming TF-IDF, Word2Vec, GloVe, LLM prompt based classification, and feature-only baselines. The proposed feature set enhances interpretability by highlighting salient linguistic cues such as second-person pronouns, superlatives, numerals, and attention-oriented punctuation, enabling transparent and well-calibrated clickbait predictions. We release code and trained models to support reproducible research.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **hybrid detection architecture** that integrates transformer-based text embeddings with 15 **linguistically motivated informativeness features**.
- Utilizes **XGBoost tree-based classifiers** to combine dense vector representations with explicit cues like superlatives and numerals for improved performance.
- Demonstrates that **feature-augmented embeddings** provide superior interpretability and calibration compared to standalone LLM prompt-based classification.
- Releases open-source code and models to facilitate **reproducible research** in automated content moderation and information quality.
key_results: Achieved a peak F1-score of 91% using XGBoost over augmented LLM embeddings.
Outperformed traditional baselines including TF-IDF, Word2Vec, and GloVe.
Surpassed zero-shot and few-shot LLM prompt-based classification accuracy.
Identified second-person pronouns, superlatives, and numerals as high-impact linguistic cues.
Enhanced model transparency through explicit feature engineering alongside neural embeddings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that combining transformer embeddings with linguistic features in XGBoost models outperforms standalone LLMs for clickbait detection.

title: A Deep Surrogate Model for Robust and Generalizable Long-Term Blast Wave Prediction
summary: Accurately modeling the spatio-temporal dynamics of blast wave propagation remains a longstanding challenge due to its highly nonlinear behavior, sharp gradients, and burdensome computational cost. While machine learning-based surrogate models offer fast inference as a promising alternative, they suffer from degraded accuracy, particularly evaluated on complex urban layouts or out-of-distribution scenarios. Moreover, autoregressive prediction strategies in such models are prone to error accumulation over long forecasting horizons, limiting their robustness for extended-time simulations. To address these limitations, we propose RGD-Blast, a robust and generalizable deep surrogate model for high-fidelity, long-term blast wave forecasting. RGD-Blast incorporates a multi-scale module to capture both global flow patterns and local boundary interactions, effectively mitigating error accumulation during autoregressive prediction. We introduce a dynamic-static feature coupling mechanism that fuses time-varying pressure fields with static source and layout features, thereby enhancing out-of-distribution generalization. Experiments demonstrate that RGD-Blast achieves a two-order-of-magnitude speedup over traditional numerical methods while maintaining comparable accuracy. In generalization tests on unseen building layouts, the model achieves an average RMSE below 0.01 and an R2 exceeding 0.89 over 280 consecutive time steps. Additional evaluations under varying blast source locations and explosive charge weights further validate its generalization, substantially advancing the state of the art in long-term blast wave modeling.

- Proposes **RGD-Blast**, a deep surrogate model designed for high-fidelity, long-term **spatio-temporal blast wave forecasting** in complex urban environments.
- Integrates a **multi-scale module** to capture global flows and local boundary interactions, effectively mitigating **autoregressive error accumulation**.
- Implements a **dynamic-static feature coupling** mechanism that fuses temporal pressure fields with static source and layout features for enhanced **OOD generalization**.
- Delivers a two-order-of-magnitude speedup over traditional **numerical methods** while maintaining comparable accuracy over 280 time steps.
key_results: Achieved a 100x speedup compared to traditional numerical simulation methods.
Maintained an average RMSE below 0.01 over long-term forecasting horizons.
Realized R-squared (R2) values exceeding 0.89 on unseen building layouts.
Demonstrated robust generalization across varying blast source locations and charge weights.
Successfully reduced error accumulation in long-horizon autoregressive predictions via multi-scale feature extraction.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce RGD-Blast, a multi-scale surrogate model achieving 100x faster, high-fidelity blast wave simulations across complex environments.

title: Unifying Formal Explanations: A Complexity-Theoretic Perspective
summary: Previous work has explored the computational complexity of deriving two fundamental types of explanations for ML model predictions: (1) *sufficient reasons*, which are subsets of input features that, when fixed, determine a prediction, and (2) *contrastive reasons*, which are subsets of input features that, when modified, alter a prediction. Prior studies have examined these explanations in different contexts, such as non-probabilistic versus probabilistic frameworks and local versus global settings. In this study, we introduce a unified framework for analyzing these explanations, demonstrating that they can all be characterized through the minimization of a unified probabilistic value function. We then prove that the complexity of these computations is influenced by three key properties of the value function: (1) *monotonicity*, (2) *submodularity*, and (3) *supermodularity* - which are three fundamental properties in *combinatorial optimization*. Our findings uncover some counterintuitive results regarding the nature of these properties within the explanation settings examined. For instance, although the *local* value functions do not exhibit monotonicity or submodularity/supermodularity whatsoever, we demonstrate that the *global* value functions do possess these properties. This distinction enables us to prove a series of novel polynomial-time results for computing various explanations with provable guarantees in the global explainability setting, across a range of ML models that span the interpretability spectrum, such as neural networks, decision trees, and tree ensembles. In contrast, we show that even highly simplified versions of these explanations become NP-hard to compute in the corresponding local explainability setting.

processed.summary:
* Introduces a **unified probabilistic value function** framework that categorizes both **sufficient reasons** and **contrastive reasons** for ML model predictions.
* Identifies that the computational complexity of generating explanations is fundamentally tied to three combinatorial properties: **monotonicity**, **submodularity**, and **supermodularity**.
* Establishes a rigorous distinction between **local and global explainability**, proving that global settings often permit efficient optimization.
* Develops **polynomial-time algorithms** for global explanations that provide provable guarantees across diverse architectures, including **neural networks** and **tree ensembles**.
key_results: Unified sufficient and contrastive explanations under a single probabilistic value function framework.
Proved that local explanation value functions generally lack monotonicity and submodularity.
Demonstrated that global value functions possess properties enabling efficient combinatorial optimization.
Identified that local explanation tasks remain NP-hard even under highly simplified conditions.
Provided novel poly-time results for global explainability across the full interpretability spectrum.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers prove that global model explanations are computationally tractable while local explanations remain NP-hard using combinatorial optimization.

title: FENCE: A Financial and Multimodal Jailbreak Detection Dataset
summary: Jailbreaking poses a significant risk to the deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs). VLMs are particularly vulnerable because they process both text and images, creating broader attack surfaces. However, available resources for jailbreak detection are scarce, particularly in finance. To address this gap, we present FENCE, a bilingual (Korean-English) multimodal dataset for training and evaluating jailbreak detectors in financial applications. FENCE emphasizes domain realism through finance-relevant queries paired with image-grounded threats. Experiments with commercial and open-source VLMs reveal consistent vulnerabilities, with GPT-4o showing measurable attack success rates and open-source models displaying greater exposure. A baseline detector trained on FENCE achieves 99 percent in-distribution accuracy and maintains strong performance on external benchmarks, underscoring the dataset's robustness for training reliable detection models. FENCE provides a focused resource for advancing multimodal jailbreak detection in finance and for supporting safer, more reliable AI systems in sensitive domains. Warning: This paper includes example data that may be offensive.

processed.summary:
- Introduction of **FENCE**, a bilingual (Korean-English) dataset designed for **multimodal jailbreak detection** in financial applications.
- Addresses critical vulnerabilities in **Vision Language Models (VLMs)** by integrating finance-relevant queries with adversarial **image-grounded threats**.
- Provides a robust benchmark for **AI Evaluation**, revealing that while GPT-4o has measurable success rates, open-source models remain highly exposed.
- Demonstrates high-performance safety filtering with a baseline detector achieving **99% accuracy**, enhancing reliability for sensitive financial deployments.

Found that GPT-4o exhibits measurable attack success rates, while open-source models show even greater vulnerability.
A baseline detector trained on FENCE achieved 99% in-distribution accuracy.
Validated model robustness through strong performance on external multimodal benchmarks.
Identified image-grounded threats as a primary expanded attack surface for financial Vision Language Models.

title: The Statistical Signature of LLMs
summary: Large language models generate text through probabilistic sampling from high-dimensional distributions, yet how this process reshapes the structural statistical organization of language remains incompletely characterized. Here we show that lossless compression provides a simple, model-agnostic measure of statistical regularity that differentiates generative regimes directly from surface text. We analyze compression behavior across three progressively more complex information ecosystems: controlled human-LLM continuations, generative mediation of a knowledge infrastructure (Wikipedia vs. Grokipedia), and fully synthetic social interaction environments (Moltbook vs. Reddit). Across settings, compression reveals a persistent structural signature of probabilistic generation. In controlled and mediated contexts, LLM-produced language exhibits higher structural regularity and compressibility than human-written text, consistent with a concentration of output within highly recurrent statistical patterns. However, this signature shows scale dependence: in fragmented interaction environments the separation attenuates, suggesting a fundamental limit to surface-level distinguishability at small scales. This compressibility-based separation emerges consistently across models, tasks, and domains and can be observed directly from surface text without relying on model internals or semantic evaluation. Overall, our findings introduce a simple and robust framework for quantifying how generative systems reshape textual production, offering a structural perspective on the evolving complexity of communication.

processed.summary:
• The paper introduces a **model-agnostic framework** that uses lossless compression to identify a distinct statistical signature in text produced by large language models.
 • It demonstrates that LLM-generated language exhibits significantly higher **structural regularity** and compressibility than human-written text, regardless of the underlying architecture.
 • The research highlights how generative systems reshape textual production by concentrating outputs within **highly recurrent statistical patterns**.
 • Findings reveal a **scale-dependent limit** to distinguishability, where the statistical separation between AI and human text attenuates in fragmented or short-form social interactions.
key_results: LLM-generated text is consistently more compressible than human text across multiple domains.
The statistical signature of generative AI persists across controlled, mediated, and synthetic environments.
Lossless compression provides a robust metric for AI detection without requiring access to model internals.
Distinguishability between human and AI text decreases as the scale of the interaction fragment shrinks.
Synthetic knowledge bases (e.g., Grokipedia) show fundamental structural differences from human-curated counterparts like Wikipedia.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLM-generated text possesses a unique statistical signature detectable through higher levels of lossless compressibility.

title: Rethinking Beam Management: Generalization Limits Under Hardware Heterogeneity
summary: Hardware heterogeneity across diverse user devices poses new challenges for beam-based communication in 5G and beyond. This heterogeneity limits the applicability of machine learning (ML)-based algorithms. This article highlights the critical need to treat hardware heterogeneity as a first-class design concern in ML-aided beam management. We analyze key failure modes in the presence of heterogeneity and present case studies demonstrating their performance impact. Finally, we discuss potential strategies to improve generalization in beam management.

processed.summary:
* The study addresses the challenge of **hardware heterogeneity** in 5G/6G beam management, where diverse user equipment configurations degrade the performance of **ML-based algorithms**.
* It identifies critical **failure modes** in ML models that assume hardware homogeneity, resulting in significant **generalization limits** during real-world deployment across different device types.
* The authors propose a shift toward **hardware-aware design**, emphasizing that device-specific constraints must be integrated into the ML architecture to maintain high throughput and reliability.
key_results: Identification of specific failure modes triggered by unseen device-side hardware variations.
Quantitative evidence that hardware-agnostic ML models suffer from out-of-distribution generalization errors.
Case studies demonstrating significant throughput loss in heterogeneous 5G environments.
Proposed framework for integrating hardware-specific parameters as first-class ML design inputs.
Strategic roadmap for improving robustness in beam management across diverse user device ecosystems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Northeastern University researchers demonstrate that hardware heterogeneity severely limits the generalization of ML-based beam management in wireless communications.

title: Stable Long-Horizon Spatiotemporal Prediction on Meshes Using Latent Multiscale Recurrent Graph Neural Networks
summary: Accurate long-horizon prediction of spatiotemporal fields on complex geometries is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning, with applications such as additive manufacturing where temperature histories govern defect formation and mechanical properties. High-fidelity simulations are accurate but computationally costly, and despite recent advances, machine learning methods remain challenged by long-horizon temperature and gradient prediction. We propose a deep learning framework for predicting full temperature histories directly on meshes, conditioned on geometry and process parameters, while maintaining stability over thousands of time steps and generalizing across heterogeneous geometries. The framework adopts a temporal multiscale architecture composed of two coupled models operating at complementary time scales. Both models rely on a latent recurrent graph neural network to capture spatiotemporal dynamics on meshes, while a variational graph autoencoder provides a compact latent representation that reduces memory usage and improves training stability. Experiments on simulated powder bed fusion data demonstrate accurate and temporally stable long-horizon predictions across diverse geometries, outperforming existing baseline. Although evaluated in two dimensions, the framework is general and extensible to physics-driven systems with multiscale dynamics and to three-dimensional geometries.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **temporal multiscale architecture** using two coupled models to capture dynamics across complementary time scales.
* Integrates a **Latent Recurrent Graph Neural Network (LRGNN)** with a **Variational Graph Autoencoder** for memory-efficient, stable mesh-based predictions.
* Focuses on **Scientific Machine Learning (SciML)** applications, specifically addressing the challenge of long-horizon temperature and gradient forecasting.
* Ensures **high stability** over thousands of inference steps while generalizing to diverse, heterogeneous geometries.
key_results: Maintains prediction stability across thousands of sequential time steps.
Significantly reduces memory usage via variational latent representations.
Generalizes effectively to unseen 2D mesh geometries.
Outperforms existing graph-based baselines in spatiotemporal accuracy.
Framework remains extensible to 3D geometries and multiscale physical systems.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a latent multiscale recurrent GNN framework for stable, long-horizon spatiotemporal field prediction on meshes.

title: Detecting Contextual Hallucinations in LLMs with Frequency-Aware Attention
summary: Hallucination detection is critical for ensuring the reliability of large language models (LLMs) in context-based generation. Prior work has explored intrinsic signals available during generation, among which attention offers a direct view of grounding behavior. However, existing approaches typically rely on coarse summaries that fail to capture fine-grained instabilities in attention. Inspired by signal processing, we introduce a frequency-aware perspective on attention by analyzing its variation during generation. We model attention distributions as discrete signals and extract high-frequency components that reflect rapid local changes in attention. Our analysis reveals that hallucinated tokens are associated with high-frequency attention energy, reflecting fragmented and unstable grounding behavior. Based on this insight, we develop a lightweight hallucination detector using high-frequency attention features. Experiments on the RAGTruth and HalluRAG benchmarks show that our approach achieves performance gains over verification-based, internal-representation-based, and attention-based methods across models and tasks.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **frequency-aware attention** analysis framework to identify contextual hallucinations by treating attention distributions as discrete signals.
• Leverages **high-frequency energy** components to detect fragmented and unstable grounding behavior that typically precedes hallucinated token generation.
• Implements a **lightweight detector** that captures fine-grained instabilities in attention distributions, moving beyond coarse summaries used in prior work.
• Demonstrates superior performance on **RAGTruth** and **HalluRAG** benchmarks compared to verification-based and internal-representation-based methods.
key_results: Hallucinated tokens are strongly associated with high-frequency attention energy.
The frequency-aware method outperforms traditional attention-based summaries in detection accuracy.
Proposed detector shows consistent gains across multiple LLM architectures and tasks.
High-frequency components effectively isolate unstable grounding behavior from stable context use.
Achieves higher reliability than internal-representation-based detectors on RAG benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a frequency-aware attention method that detects LLM hallucinations by identifying unstable, high-frequency grounding signals.

title: Toward Automated Virtual Electronic Control Unit (ECU) Twins for Shift-Left Automotive Software Testing
summary: Automotive software increasingly outpaces hardware availability, forcing late integration and expensive hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) bottlenecks. The InnoRegioChallenge project investigated whether a virtual test and integration environment can reproduce electronic control unit (ECU) behavior early enough to run real software binaries before physical hardware exists. We report a prototype that generates instruction-accurate processor models in SystemC/TLM~2.0 using an agentic, feedback-driven workflow coupled to a reference simulator via the GNU Debugger (GDB). The results indicate that the most critical technical risk -- CPU behavioral fidelity -- can be reduced through automated differential testing and iterative model correction. We summarize the architecture, the agentic modeling loop, and project outcomes, and we extrapolate plausible technical details consistent with the reported qualitative findings. While cloud-scale deployment and full toolchain integration remain future work, the prototype demonstrates a viable shift-left path for virtual ECU twins, enabling reproducible tests, non-intrusive tracing, and fault-injection campaigns aligned with safety standards.

processed.summary:
* The framework introduces an **agentic, feedback-driven workflow** to automate the generation of instruction-accurate virtual ECU twins in **SystemC/TLM 2.0**.
* It utilizes **differential testing** via the **GNU Debugger (GDB)** to synchronize virtual models with reference simulators for iterative model correction and validation.
* The architecture enables **shift-left testing** by allowing real software binaries to run in a virtual environment before physical hardware is manufactured.
key_results: Successful automation of instruction-accurate processor model generation using agentic loops.
Significant reduction in CPU behavioral fidelity risks through automated iterative refinement.
Support for non-intrusive tracing and fault-injection campaigns compliant with safety standards.
Elimination of hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) bottlenecks by providing early virtual integration.
Demonstration of a viable pathway for cloud-scale deployment of automotive virtual twins.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The InnoRegioChallenge project develops an agentic workflow to automate virtual ECU twins, enabling early, hardware-independent automotive software testing.

title: Advection-Diffusion on Graphs: A Bakry-Emery Laplacian for Spectral Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) often struggle to propagate information across long distances due to oversmoothing and oversquashing. Existing remedies such as graph transformers or rewiring typically incur high computational cost or require altering the graph structure. We introduce a Bakry-Emery graph Laplacian that integrates diffusion and advection through a learnable node-wise potential, inducing task-dependent propagation dynamics without modifying topology. This operator has a well-behaved spectral decomposition and acts as a drop-in replacement for standard Laplacians in spectral GNNs. Building on this insight, we develop mu-ChebNet, a spectral architecture that jointly learns the potential and Chebyshev filters, effectively bridging message-passing adaptivity and spectral efficiency. Our theoretical analysis shows how the potential modulates the spectrum, enabling control of key graph properties. Empirically, mu-ChebNet delivers consistent gains on synthetic long-range reasoning tasks, as well as real-world benchmarks, while offering an interpretable routing field that reveals how information flows through the graph. This establishes the Bakry-Emery Laplacian as a principled and efficient foundation for adaptive spectral graph learning.

processed.summary:
• Introduces the **Bakry-Emery graph Laplacian**, a novel operator that integrates **advection and diffusion** through a learnable node-wise potential to solve **oversmoothing and oversquashing**.
• Proposes **mu-ChebNet**, a spectral GNN architecture that jointly learns the potential and **Chebyshev filters**, effectively bridging message-passing adaptivity with spectral efficiency.
• Enables **task-dependent propagation dynamics** without modifying graph topology, offering a cheaper alternative to graph transformers or structural rewiring.
• Provides a principled foundation for **adaptive spectral learning**, featuring a well-behaved spectral decomposition and an interpretable routing field for information flow.

Achieved consistent performance gains on synthetic long-range reasoning benchmarks.

title: Cavity Solitons as a Nonlinear Substrate for Photonic Neuromorphic Computing
summary: Reservoir computing leverages nonlinear dynamics of physical systems to process temporal information with minimal training cost. Here, we demonstrate that cavity solitons sustained in a fiber optical cavity provide an optical platform for photonic reservoir computing. Our methodology exploits the use of a phase-modulated drive laser to encode the input, while the reservoir states are accessed through frequency-resolved readout. Numerical simulations indicate that the emission of Kelly waves enriches the dynamics and enhances performance for machine learning tasks. We evaluate the performance of the cavity-soliton reservoir computer on several standard benchmark tasks.

- Proposes a **Photonic Reservoir Computing** (PRC) architecture that utilizes **cavity solitons** within fiber optical cavities as a nonlinear medium.
- Employs a **phase-modulated drive laser** for temporal input encoding and a **frequency-resolved readout** system for state extraction.
- Demonstrates that the emission of **Kelly waves** enriches the system's nonlinear dynamics, significantly improving performance on ML tasks.
- Offers a low-training-cost alternative for temporal information processing by leveraging inherent **optical dynamics**.

Identification of Kelly wave emission as a key factor in enhancing computational richness.
Successful implementation of phase-modulated drive lasers for efficient input encoding.

Establishment of frequency-resolved readout as an effective method for reservoir state access.

title: TempoNet: Slack-Quantized Transformer-Guided Reinforcement Scheduler for Adaptive Deadline-Centric Real-Time Dispatchs
summary: Real-time schedulers must reason about tight deadlines under strict compute budgets. We present TempoNet, a reinforcement learning scheduler that pairs a permutation-invariant Transformer with a deep Q-approximation. An Urgency Tokenizer discretizes temporal slack into learnable embeddings, stabilizing value learning and capturing deadline proximity. A latency-aware sparse attention stack with blockwise top-k selection and locality-sensitive chunking enables global reasoning over unordered task sets with near-linear scaling and sub-millisecond inference. A multicore mapping layer converts contextualized Q-scores into processor assignments through masked-greedy selection or differentiable matching. Extensive evaluations on industrial mixed-criticality traces and large multiprocessor settings show consistent gains in deadline fulfillment over analytic schedulers and neural baselines, together with improved optimization stability. Diagnostics include sensitivity analyses for slack quantization, attention-driven policy interpretation, hardware-in-the-loop and kernel micro-benchmarks, and robustness under stress with simple runtime mitigations; we also report sample-efficiency benefits from behavioral-cloning pretraining and compatibility with an actor-critic variant without altering the inference pipeline. These results establish a practical framework for Transformer-based decision making in high-throughput real-time scheduling.

processed.summary:
• **TempoNet** utilizes a reinforcement learning framework that combines a **permutation-invariant Transformer** with deep Q-approximation for real-time task scheduling.
• The system incorporates a novel **Urgency Tokenizer** to discretize temporal slack into learnable embeddings, significantly stabilizing the value learning process.
• Its **latency-aware sparse attention** stack employs blockwise top-k selection and locality-sensitive chunking to maintain sub-millisecond inference and near-linear scaling.
• A specialized multicore mapping layer converts contextualized scores into processor assignments using **masked-greedy selection** or differentiable matching strategies.
key_results: Outperformed analytic and neural scheduling baselines in deadline fulfillment rates.
Achieved sub-millisecond inference speeds suitable for high-throughput dispatching.
Demonstrated superior optimization stability via slack-quantized urgency embeddings.
Showed significant sample-efficiency gains through behavioral-cloning pretraining.
Maintained robustness under stress with near-linear scaling on large multiprocessor traces.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TempoNet, a Transformer-based RL scheduler that optimizes real-time task deadlines using sparse attention and slack-quantization.

title: A contour for the entanglement negativity of bosonic Gaussian states
summary: We construct a contour function for the logarithmic negativity and the logarithm of the moments of the partial transpose of the reduced density matrix for multimode bosonic Gaussian states of a free lattice model. In one spatial dimension, numerical results are obtained for harmonic chains either in the ground state or at finite temperature, by considering, respectively, either a subsystem made by two adjacent or disjoint blocks on the line or a bipartition of the circle. The contour function of the logarithmic negativity diverges only at the entangling points, while the contour function for the logarithm of the moments of the partial transpose is divergent also at the boundary of the bipartite subsystem, as functions of the position. In a two-dimensional conformal field theory, analytic expressions that describe these divergencies are discussed. In one spatial dimension, we explore the partial derivative of the logarithmic negativity of two adjacent intervals with respect to the logarithm of the harmonic ratio of their lengths while their ratio and the other parameters are kept fixed. Considering the ground state of the harmonic chain on the line and in the massive regime, we report numerical results showing that this quantity displays a monotonically decreasing behaviour.

title: Fair Orientations: Proportionality and Equitability
summary: We study the fair allocation of indivisible items under relevance constraints, where each agent has a set of relevant items and can only receive items that are relevant to them. While the relevance constraint has been studied in recent years, existing work has largely focused on envy-freeness. Our work extends this study to other key fairness criteria -- such as proportionality, equitability, and their relaxations -- in settings where the items may be goods, chores, or a mixture of both. We complement the literature by presenting a picture of the existence and computational complexity of the considered criteria.

 • Analyzes the existence and **computational complexity** of various fairness metrics, specifically focusing on **proportionality (Prop)** and **equitability (EQ)**.
 • Establishes a theoretical framework for **fair orientations**, providing a comprehensive map of feasibility for relaxed criteria such as Prop1 and EQ1 in multi-agent environments.

title: Neurosymbolic Language Reasoning as Satisfiability Modulo Theory
summary: Natural language understanding requires interleaving textual and logical reasoning, yet large language models often fail to perform such reasoning reliably. Existing neurosymbolic systems combine LLMs with solvers but remain limited to fully formalizable tasks such as math or program synthesis, leaving natural documents with only partial logical structure unaddressed. We introduce Logitext, a neurosymbolic language that represents documents as natural language text constraints (NLTCs), making partial logical structure explicit. We develop an algorithm that integrates LLM-based constraint evaluation with satisfiability modulo theory (SMT) solving, enabling joint textual-logical reasoning. Experiments on a new content moderation benchmark, together with LegalBench and Super-Natural Instructions, show that Logitext improves both accuracy and coverage. This work is the first that treats LLM-based reasoning as an SMT theory, extending neurosymbolic methods beyond fully formalizable domains.

- Developed an algorithm that treats **LLM-based constraint evaluation as an SMT theory**, allowing formal solvers to interleave textual interpretation with symbolic logic.
- Extends the reach of neurosymbolic methods beyond rigid domains like math or code to **semi-structured natural language** tasks such as legal analysis.
